text: The surgeon identifies the portion of the small bowel for resection and uses a harmonic scalpel to enter the mesenteric border, creating a window through to the other side. The harmonic scalpel is employed to manage small vessels and prevent bleeding in the area.

The surgeon manipulates the small bowel over the energy device to assess the size and location of the created window. A linear stapling device is positioned through the window, and the small bowel is adjusted to fit within the jaws of the stapler. The device is angled away from the mesenteric border towards the anti-mesenteric border to ensure adequate perfusion during resection.

The surgeon uses a linear stapling device, placing the tine through the mesenteric border of the small bowel. The device is articulated to achieve an optimal angle for preserving blood supply to the far corner opposite the specimen side.

The surgeon uses an energy device to transect the mesentery, ensuring cauterization of smaller vessels along the mesenteric border to prevent bleeding. The specimen is removed, and the staple line is entered at the anti-mesenteric corner, which has minimal blood supply.

The surgeon aligns the two sections of the small bowel adjacent to each other at the anti-mesenteric border, using the left hand to position the bowel across both tines of the stapler. After removing the stapler, the staple line is checked, and preparations are made for suture repair.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture in a running fashion, starting at the apex and continuing to the staple line to close the aneurotomy. An additional suture may be placed in a limber fashion to reinforce the suture line.

The surgeon ties the suture in place, addressing the mesenteric defect. In oncologic resections, this defect may be larger. The omentum is draped over the small bowel to restore its natural anatomical position.
